title: Stratus Computer Corporate Overview, November 1982
author: Stratus Computer, Inc. (corporate marketing — Kastner-era artifact)
abstract: Stratus Computer Corporate Overview dated November 1982, prepared at the company's 17-19 Strathmore Road, Natick MA headquarters. Document covers business goals, market targets, marketing strategy, human resources, financial resources, customers, and product overview (Continuous Processing architecture, modular expansion, serviceability, multiprocessor technology, software). States Stratus founded May 1980 to design/manufacture/market supermini-class computers for OLTP applications where high availability is paramount. Names founders William E. Foster (Pres., ex-Data General), Robert A. Freiburghouse (VP Software, ex-Honeywell Multics), Gardner C. Hendrie (VP Hardware, ex-Data General); also John P. Morgridge (VP Marketing), James E.D. Austin (VP Manufacturing), John H. Curtis (VP Finance). Notes March 1982 multi-year agreement with Olivetti for European/South American/African marketing. Sales offices established in Boston, Chicago, Dallas, Houston, Los Angeles, New York/New Jersey, San Francisco, Tampa, Washington DC. Target market: Fortune 1000 OLTP applications. Cites Cowen/Datamation 1982 minicomputer survey ranking hardware reliability as #1 selection factor. Includes forecast of high-availability transaction-processing market growth from $6.4B (1981) to $27B (1986) per Information Corp 9/82. Kastner joined Stratus in 1981 as part of marketing/sales support team — this document is from his employer-era period and reflects the company's self-positioning during that chapter.
importance_rationale: Foundational corporate document of Stratus during Kastner's marketing tenure; captures founder lineage (HP/Honeywell/Data General), the OLTP/high-availability thesis, and early Olivetti international distribution agreement. Critical context for Kastner's Stratus chapter and for understanding the early fault-tolerant computing market vs. Tandem.
relevance_rationale: Continuous Processing architecture (hardware-redundant pair-and-spare) and the OLTP-availability thesis remain central in modern always-on systems (financial markets, telecom, government). Stratus the company persists; the specific product line is mature/legacy.
prescience_rationale: Predicted that 'businesses and institutions of all sizes will put the key functions of their operations on-line via a computer system or network' — pre-Internet, this anticipated the universal connectivity of modern commerce. Forecasted 20-40% growth for terminals, superminis, 32-bit minis in OLTP and OEM/end-user CDP markets. The directional call (online migration of all business functions) was completely correct.
